Dlicom is a SocialFi app that combines a private messenger, a crypto wallet, voice and video calls, and a built in earning system in one place.

The core idea is a seamless flow. You chat. You send crypto. You farm rewards for activity. You stake. You pay for premium features. You receive ad rewards. You participate in governance. No switching between apps. Everything lives inside one intuitive interface.

Key features.
Encrypted voice and video calls with end to end encryption.
A multichain wallet built directly into the app.
In chat crypto transfers inside conversations.
Zero knowledge privacy to protect user data.
The DLI token, an ERC 20 on Base, used for SFR staking, tipping, ad payments, premium features, and governance.

Tokenomics according to the official site.
Total supply 355 million.
Initial circulating supply around 82 million.
Presale price 0.05 dollars.
Initial market cap around 4.1 million dollars. Fully diluted valuation around 17.75 million dollars.
Liquidity 15 percent. Public presale 14.08 percent. Community incentives 3 percent.
Deflationary model with buybacks and burns funded from fees.

The project is currently in an active phase.
The app is already live with thousands of users.
The presale is ongoing with more than 4 million USDC raised.
Discord is open.
A Regional Lead Hunt is live with paid positions for leaders in regions such as India, Russia, Vietnam, Ukraine, and others.

Personally, I like the approach. It is not just hype around airdrops. It focuses on real utility, privacy, monetization, and convenience in one product. With the presale and community building in full swing, this is an interesting early stage to watch, especially if OG roles and Regional Leads come with strong incentives.

In the endless digital cosmos, a lonely blue sphere named Dili drifted among the cold nodes of the blockchain. He knew that somewhere there were others, but each was locked in its own centralized cage with no real contact and no freedom to share value. One day Dili noticed a pink sphere, Dlicom, just like him, but surrounded by a swarm of green chaos monsters: censoring algorithms, parasitic ads, and spy bots that stole its light and data. Dili did not hesitate. He activated the pure code of freedom, a decentralized communication protocol, and rushed to help. His light scattered the monsters and an encrypted channel instantly connected the two spheres directly with no intermediaries, no surveillance, and no borders.Since then, Dili and Dlicom have been flying together through the Web3 universe. They are not just friends. They build bridges. They help lonely spheres create secure chats, send value such as tokens, tines, and love with a single touch, earn together, and grow in a real community. And every time someone feels cut off from the world, they appear nearby and whisper:
You are not alone. Connect. Protect what is yours. Share freely. Here your data is yours, your connections are real, your value is only yours.All it takes is courage to open the channel and you are no longer alone. You are part of Dlicom.

#Web3 #Dlicom #CommunityBuilding

New SocialFi project that’s trying to put everything in one place: chat, social interaction, earning, transactions, and even mini-apps inside the platform.

The main idea is that a regular Telegram chat turns into a full-fledged ecosystem where you communicate, farm points/tokens for activity, trade, receive drops from partners — all without leaving the app. Basically, a kind of SuperApp inside Telegram, but with a crypto economy and SocialFi mechanics.

They’ve just opened their Discord and are giving out 1,000 OG roles to early participants. Anyone who joins, gets the role, and stays active will receive rewards (they promise to reveal the details inside the server soon). This is their first public step toward community building.

For now, the project is still young: the whitepaper isn’t released, the token hasn’t launched, and the roadmap isn’t detailed yet. However, the team clearly wants to grow a user base quickly before a major announcement.

If the OG roles end up giving real benefits (airdrop, early access, higher rewards), it could be interesting.

I opened the Figma file from Dlicom and honestly was surprised. They clearly invested serious effort into branding.

Everything looks polished and cohesive. Cute robots with different emotions. Clean blue gradients. Minimalistic icons. The characters are recognizable. The color palette is calm but still has personality. The typography feels modern and readable.

To me, that is a strong signal. If a team at an early stage is already paying this much attention to visual identity, it usually means they are thinking long term. In SocialFi, many projects focus only on token plus farming mechanics. Here, at least the design language says we want people to actually enjoy using this product.

The robots especially stand out. They do not look like generic AI art. They feel expressive and alive.

Based on those robot characters, I created a couple of stickers that can be used in conversations.

A few days back, I got OG in @DlicomApp’s DC server. At the time when OG camping was going on, there wasn’t this much hype.

Recently I’ve been seeing a lot of discussion about it, so instead of just going with the hype, I properly did my own research.

There is a big centralization problem in social media + finance.

Right now, most platforms are controlled by big tech companies. Your data gets collected, conversations are moderated opaquely, ads use you but you don’t get fair rewards, and financial tools are often custodial or spread across different apps.

Privacy? Mostly secondary.
Ownership? Almost nothing.
The entire value flows upward to corporations, and the user becomes the “product.”

This is where @DlicomApp comes in.

They are building an all in one decentralized SocialFi ecosystem that gives control back to users — secure messaging, social networking, self-custody wallet, staking, in-chat transactions and more — all seamlessly integrated on a single platform.

Instead of using separate apps (one for chat, one for wallet, one for social), Dlicom combines everything into one privacy-first dApp built on blockchain (on scalable layers like Base and ZKsync).

In their model, users get:

• True data ownership and privacy (end-to-end encryption, no silent data harvesting)
• Self-custody wallet, manage assets, swaps, P2P payments
• Tokenized engagement earn through ads, staking rewards, and community activity via $DLI token
• Censorship resistance and decentralization, no single entity controlling everything
• AI assisted moderation, keeping things clean without big tech overreach

What is drawing attention:

– All in one hub: No need to switch apps for private chat, transactions and earning
– Strong focus on privacy + ownership in a world where AI and companies are heavily harvesting data
– Utility-driven tokenomics ($DLI powers fees, rewards and governance)

Let’s talk about how 45% Ad Revenue Share changes the game on Dlicom 👇

Most traditional social platforms follow one simple formula:

Users create content.
Users generate attention.
Advertisers pay.
Platform keeps the profit.

The value flows in one direction.

User attention → Platform revenue.

Creators fuel the ecosystem, but rarely participate in the upside.

Now here’s where Dlicom flips the model.

Instead of capturing 100% of advertising revenue internally, the platform allocates ~45% back to users and creators.

Let’s break that down practically:

• Content gets created
• Users engage
• Advertisers pay for visibility
• Revenue is partially redistributed

The loop doesn’t end at the platform.

It extends back to the community.

Traditional model: Users → Attention → Platform profit

Dlicom model: Users → Engagement → Ad revenue → Shared distribution

That shift changes incentives.

Creators are rewarded for meaningful content.
Users benefit from active participation.
Growth becomes community-aligned.

Of course, let’s stay realistic.

Revenue sharing only works if: 

• The platform attracts real users
• Advertisers see value
• Activity keeps compounding

Without growth, redistribution is just theory.

But the principle is powerful:

Attention shouldn’t only be extracted.
It should be shared.

That’s the experiment Dlicom is running.

And if it scales, SocialFi monetization may never look the same again.

sending cryptocurrency through a chat interface without copying wallet addresses solves one of the most persistent friction points in peer to peer transfers. dlicom embeds crypto transfers directly into its messaging layer, so the recipient is already identified by account context rather than a raw address string. this removes a common source of user error and address substitution attacks.

the platform combines messaging with a self custody wallet that handles gas fees using the native dli token, removing the need to hold separate gas reserves across multiple chains. it also functions as a dex aggregator, routing swaps internally to reduce slippage and mev exposure without connecting to external exchanges. this keeps the full transaction flow inside a single interface.

staking rewards are tied to platform revenue rather than token emissions, and holders can vote on fee structures, feature additions, and staking parameters. content creators receive dli directly through tipping and an onchain attention model where users are compensated for engaging with ads. the design consolidates functions that currently require several separate tools into one app.

@DlicomApp

most messaging apps treat financial transactions as an afterthought, bolted on after the fact through third party integrations that require users to leave the conversation entirely. dlicom takes a different architectural approach, building a self custody wallet directly into the communication layer so that sending cryptocurrency happens within the same interface as sending a message, with no context switching or external app required.

the wallet itself functions as a dex aggregator, meaning token swaps are routed across multiple decentralized exchanges simultaneously to find the best available price and minimize slippage. this is meaningful in practice because it removes the manual labor of comparing rates across platforms, and it reduces exposure to evm bot activity that often front runs trades submitted directly to a single exchange.

the platform runs on ethereum and incorporates zero knowledge proofs for privacy, using zeeve as the zk infrastructure provider. this means users can verify ownership or eligibility for certain actions without exposing the underlying data, which is relevant for governance participation and access gating to premium features.

the native token, dli, is used to pay gas fees within the app, which simplifies one of the more friction heavy parts of using ethereum based applications. rather than maintaining a separate eth balance just to cover transaction costs, users operate within a single token economy that covers messaging, swaps, governance voting, and access to tools like the market screener and portfolio tracker.

content creators on the platform receive tips in dli directly through the feed, and the platform redistributes 45 percent of ad revenue back to creators and users. combining that incentive structure with a no kyc, fully decentralized architecture positions dlicom as infrastructure for people who want financial and social tools that do not depend on centralized intermediaries to function.

How Dlicom Is Redefining Communication in Decentralized Systems

Every new wave of technology solves one visible problem and quietly exposes a deeper one.

Blockchain solved trust in transactions.
Smart contracts automated logic.
AI scaled intelligence.

But communication between systems? Between agents? Between protocols?

That part remained fragmented.

Different chains.
Different standards.
Different messaging layers.
No unified coordination.

And that’s where Dlicom came through.

The Problem No One Wanted to Talk About

As Web3 evolved, protocols became smarter but not necessarily better at speaking to each other.

Projects built powerful systems:

On-chain automation

Agent-based AI

Cross-chain applications

Decentralized finance strategies

Yet most of them relied on:

Patchwork integrations

Centralized relayers

Off-chain coordination tools

Manual triggers

The infrastructure existed.
The communication layer didn’t.

It wasn’t about speed.
It was about clarity, structure, and verifiable intent.

The Shift: Communication as Infrastructure

Dlicom didn’t approach the ecosystem as another app or token layer.

It focused on something more foundational:

Structured, verifiable, programmable communication.

Instead of asking: “How do we move assets?”

It asked: “How do systems express intent clearly and execute it reliably?”

That shift matters.

Because in decentralized environments:

Intent needs validation

Instructions need structure

Messages need accountability

Without that, automation becomes guesswork.

What Dlicom Changed

@DlicomApp introduced a communication first approach.

Not just data passing.
Not just API style calls.

But coordinated, on-chain aligned interaction between actors human or machine.

Think about it this way:

When two smart contracts interact, it shouldn’t feel like two strangers improvising.

It should feel like:

Shared standards

Defined message formats

Clear execution pathways

Predictable outcomes

Dlicom pushes toward that model.

Why This Matters Now

We are entering an era of:

AI agents executing financial strategies

Autonomous protocols managing liquidity

Multi-chain systems interacting in real time

Without a proper communication layer, complexity increases.
Risk increases.
Failure points multiply.

Dlicom positions communication not as a feature but as infrastructure.

And infrastructure is what scales ecosystems.

The Bigger Lesson

Every strong network eventually discovers the same truth:

Execution is important.
Liquidity is important.
Intelligence is important.

But coordination is what unlocks exponential growth.

Dlicom came through not by chasing hype but by addressing the quiet bottleneck.

And in decentralized systems,
the quiet bottlenecks are usually the most important ones to fix.

The Future of Social Is Intentional Communication, Not Algorithmic Noise

Most platforms today optimize for distribution.
Feeds are engineered for reach, virality, and retention.
The result? Conversations get diluted, context gets lost, and interaction becomes performance.

@DlicomApp is built differently.

Dlicom focuses on restoring intentional communication over algorithmic distribution. Instead of manipulating feeds to maximize attention, it prioritizes direct interaction where messages reach people because they are meant to, not because an algorithm decided they should.

Why This Matters

1. Signal over noise
When platforms prioritize engagement metrics, the loudest content wins. Intentional systems prioritize relevance and clarity instead.

2. Direct interaction
Communication should feel deliberate. Dlicom emphasizes purposeful exchange rather than endless scrolling.

3. Reduced feed manipulation
Algorithmic amplification often distorts conversation. By shifting focus away from feed engineering, Dlicom aims to preserve authenticity.

4. Human-centered design
Social tools should serve dialogue, not attention extraction. That principle shapes how Dlicom approaches interaction.

The Bigger Shift

We are moving from attention economies to value driven communication systems.
From performance to participation.
From manipulation to meaning.

Dlicom is part of that transition not by adding more features, but by rethinking the foundation of how people connect online.

Intentional communication isn’t a trend.
It’s the correction the digital world has been overdue for.

We were told social is about connection.

Algorithms.
Performance loops.

Engagement replaced conversation.
Distribution replaced intention.

Real social isn’t about being seen.

Dlicom - Roles hierarcy explained

Many of you were confused regarding @DlicomApp  roles and how they work, let me simplify it for you.

Dlicom has launched something called "The Regional Lead Hunt" - It’s an opportunity to either step into leadership as a Regional Lead/Helper or earn structured contribution roles as new regions go live.

As of now there are 5 regions:

India, Nigeria, Ukraine, Russia and Arab

And currently they have no Leads or helpers, which  means the field is open. 

Now let’s understand the hierarchy clearly - 

Dliever is the first foundational role - It is given to pioneers who have proven their trust, knowledge, and early contribution to Dlicom.

Dcoded is the second tier - This role represents active and consistent community members who set the standard and lead by example.

DCO (Dlicom Contribution Officer) is the highest and most exclusive role - It is for long term consistency, quality contribution within the ecosystem.

You have two clear paths.

The first path is leadership - I have told about this in my previous post.

The second path is contribution. Work your way up through Dliever → Dcoded → DCO by showing real participation.

The 2nd path is split into three phases.

Phase 1 (Feb 25 – Mar 3) focuses on observation and initial selection. Top contributors in each region can be nominated for Dliever (limited to 10 per region).

Phase 2 (Mar 4 – Mar 10) selects the top candidates per region for a test period. Only Dlievers can be upgraded to Dcoded (limited to 5 per region).

Phase 3 (Mar 11 – Mar 18) finalizes Regional Lead & Helper. This is when DCO role is assigned. You must hold Dcoded for this role (limited to only 1 per region).

Every role is manually looked into and assigned.

There are no shortcuts here. If you have been waiting for a real opportunity to step up, this is it.

Show up. Contribute. Take what you deserve.

When Conversations Move Value: The Future of In-Chat Crypto with @DlicomApp 

Social platforms were built for conversation. Crypto was built for value transfer. The problem? They’ve rarely worked in the same place at the same time.

Dlicom flips that model by turning chat into a financial layer. Instead of forcing users to leave a thread to open a wallet and complete a transaction, value moves directly inside the conversation. Sending tokens feels no different than sending a message it happens in-line, in-context, and in real time.

At the center of the ecosystem is $DLI, an ERC-20 token designed for utility across messaging, tipping, staking, and governance. Its role isn’t just speculative, it’s functional. Activity on the platform feeds into a buyback and burn system funded by ad revenue and service fees, introducing deflationary mechanics tied to real usage.

The Stake for Revenue model, launched in Q2 2025, further aligns incentives. Holders can lock DLI and earn rewards based on platform performance, not just token emissions. Infrastructure-wise, Dlicom leverages Base for scalable throughput and integrates zero-knowledge proofs to protect user data while maintaining efficiency.

This gives rise to a unified experience where communication and digital value operate as one system not two separate apps competing for attention.

Honestly, calling @DlicomApp another SocialFi app” doesn’t really capture what they’re trying to do. From what I see, this isn’t about adding crypto features to a social platform. It’s about rethinking how digital interaction should work from the ground up.
Right now, every major social platform controls your identity, your data, and ultimately your reach. Your account exists on their servers. Your visibility depends on their algorithm. Your data becomes part of their business model. If your account gets restricted or removed, there’s very little you can do.
Dlicom’s approach shifts that structure. Identity is tied to wallet ownership rather than a centralized database account. Access and control are linked to cryptographic ownership, not a company’s internal permission system. That alone changes the power dynamic between user and platform.
Another key difference is how communication and finance are integrated. Normally, you chat in one app, hold assets in another, and trade on a separate platform. Dlicom combines messaging, asset management, and on-chain interaction inside one environment. That’s not just convenience - it reduces friction, limits cross-platform exposure, and creates a more cohesive system.
On the infrastructure side, they didn’t simply deploy on an existing network and stop there. Launching their own ZK-based Layer 2 shows they’re thinking long-term about scalability and control. Owning part of the infrastructure layer gives them flexibility around upgrades, cost structure, and ecosystem expansion. Projects that control infrastructure tend to have stronger strategic positioning over time.
From a security standpoint, the model is also different. Traditional social media relies on centralized storage and custodial systems. In a wallet-linked, self-custody structure, assets and access aren’t sitting in one central vault. Risk doesn’t disappear, but responsibility shifts toward user-controlled cryptographic security, which aligns more closely with Web3 principles.
If this model executes well, Dlicom doesn’t just compete with existing social apps - it becomes more like a decentralized social operating layer. A place where identity is portable, assets are self-controlled, and communication and value exchange are native to the same system.
It’s still early, and execution will decide everything. Adoption, competition, and product quality will matter more than vision alone. But structurally, this isn’t just feature-building. It’s stack-building.
And in Web3, the projects that build the stack  not just the surface  are usually the ones worth paying attention to. 👀

In the endless streams of Web2, where social media had long ceased to be just a content feed, lived a guy named Alex. He'd spent years building his reputation: assembling teams of freelancers, launching mini-businesses, and coordinating activist communities.

"Trust is defaulted to the platform, not the users," he thought as yet another report or moderator's whim wiped his account, audience, and message history. In Web2, everything scaled quickly, but freedom remained an illusion. Advertising fed on attention, algorithms pushed emotions and polarization, and users? 

Merely fuel for the machine.

One night, amid the chaos of Twitter storms, Alex stumbled upon a manifesto: "Social networks are infrastructure: a reputation-based labor market, a media layer, a coordination tool, an economic engine." 

This was Dlicom. 

Not just the promise of Web3 with its wallets and blockchains, but a reality where digital rights are becoming inalienable. "In Web2, you rent a presence," the text read. "In Web3, you own the identity, assets, and reputation–enforced at the infrastructure level."

Alex downloaded the app. 

The heart of Dlicom was a self-custody wallet, integrated into every flow. A product with posts, stories, and reels, chats via text, voice, and video–and within the context of all this, value-driven actions: tipping, staking, transactions. Without a break in meaning. "Social networks are no longer attention factories," the narrative explained. "SocialFi transforms them into a system where value flows directly between people and communities."

Everything changed with the first post. Alex shared his idea for a startup–a platform for decentralized teams. Dlicom's AI layer came to life: moderation scanned text and images for a multitude of signals, filtering out harm. Discovery adapted to his behavior, but within the framework of security. Before every deal, insights surfaced: the wallet's reputation based on on-chain data and external reports. "Signals before errors," the interface winked.
And then Dili appeared–not just a mascot, but the voice of SocialFi. With a sly look, he materialized in the chat: 

– Hi! I'm Dili. 

Here, trust is based on verifiable rules, context is preserved, participation is transformed into value. Let's figure out why Web2 is broken: banks don't freeze accounts without explanation, but platforms can easily do that. Dlicom is changing that."

The $DLI token brought the ecosystem to life: support for creators, premium features, rewards, referrals, governance. "Participation is becoming economic," Dili proclaimed. The whitepaper revealed the tokenomics and roadmap–everything transparently, without financial advice.

The climax came when a competitor from Web2 tried to sink them with fake reports. But decentralization worked at Dlicom: there's no single actor to rewrite the rules. Dlicom opened the social economy to the world–from reputation to real money, from trust in platforms to verifiable connections.

I want to discuss one topic today that we frequently overlook. We use financial services, messaging apps, and social media on a daily basis. However, if you examine further, you will notice that we have a number of structural issues.

1. Control
Data, algorithms, and account access are centralized. Users create digital identities, but do not fully own them.

2. Monetization of attention
User activity is converted into advertising revenue. The main economic benefit remains with the platform.

3. Centralized risk
When there is a single operator, there is a single point of control. Everyone is immediately impacted by any rule changes or technical malfunctions.

There is a solution to these problems. And that solution is @DlicomApp.

The first problem is control.
At Dlicom, this issue is solved through the integration of a self-custody wallet and decentralized social infrastructure. Users control their assets and interactions without intermediaries.

The second problem is monetizing attention.
Dlicom changes the platform's economy: part of the advertising revenue is distributed between users and creators, and the DLI token is used within the ecosystem for staking and internal settlements.

The third problem is centralized risk.
In Dlicom, key mechanisms operate through blockchain and smart contracts rather than through a single operator. This means that rules and financial processes do not depend on the decisions of a single company.

So we can confidently say that Dlicom is more than just an app. It’s a comprehensive ecosystem that combines communication, finance, and its own internal economy.

I decided to take a closer look at @DlicomApp and share what I’ve found so far. I'll give a brief description of this project and why it drew my interest below.

Dlicom is a mobile product that builds an internal economy around the DLI token. Chat, social activity, and financial instruments are not separated here, but work as a single system with its own revenue model.

Upon delving deeper into this project, I noticed a few things.

The first is crypto transfers directly within the chat. You communicate and can send assets in the same window. No copying addresses or switching to other services. 

The second is the staking model. Part of the platform's income is distributed among those who stake DLI. In other words, they’re trying to connect the token to real platform income, not just speculation.

The third is the distribution of advertising revenue between users and creators. This becomes a question of scaling, but the logic itself seems well thought out: activity within the platform makes economic sense.

This is only the start. I intend to keep delving deeper into this project's analysis.
